input: Why is model.eval() not the same as torch.no_grad()?
output: Because they operate on different axes. model.eval() flips the module's training flag, which tells nn.Dropout to pass inputs through unchanged and tells nn.BatchNorm to normalize using its stored running_mean and running_var instead of the current batch. torch.no_grad() is a context manager that prevents autograd from recording operations, which is what actually stops gradient computation and frees the memory that would otherwise hold the graph. Neither one does the other's job, so during inference you wrap the forward pass in torch.no_grad() and call model.eval() on the model — they are complementary, not interchangeable.

input: Why doesn't calling model.eval() disable gradient tracking?
output: Because model.eval() only changes module-level behavior — it toggles the training flag that a few stateful layers such as nn.Dropout and nn.BatchNorm read. It does not touch autograd at all. Autograd is a separate system that records every operation regardless of the module's mode, and only torch.no_grad() can tell it to stop recording. After model.eval(), the forward pass still builds the full computation graph and still holds on to intermediate activations for potential backprop. To actually skip the graph during inference, wrap the forward pass in torch.no_grad() as a separate step.

input: Why do I need both model.eval() and torch.no_grad() for inference?
output: Because each one controls an independent thing that the other cannot reach. model.eval() is what makes nn.Dropout stop zeroing elements and what makes nn.BatchNorm use its stored running statistics instead of the current mini-batch — without it, those layers misbehave at inference time. torch.no_grad() is what stops autograd from recording the forward pass — without it, PyTorch still builds the computation graph and allocates memory for backprop even though you never call backward(). Neither flag implies the other, so a correct inference pattern uses model.eval() to fix module behavior and torch.no_grad() to skip graph construction.
